seoTitle: Create a Child Page in Confluence (2026 Guide)
metaDescription: Learn how to create a child page in Confluence in 2026, nest documents under a parent page, and manage permission inheritance for a clean sidebar tree.
intro: A cluttered sidebar full of loose, unorganized documents makes it hard to see how content relates. This guide shows how to create nested child pages in Confluence in 2026 to build a clear, indexed document hierarchy.

steps.heading: Open the parent page
steps.body: Open your designated parent page and move your cursor to the left-hand sidebar navigation panel.

steps.heading: Add a child page from the sidebar
steps.body: Hover over the parent page title and click the plus icon to drop a fresh child page instantly.

steps.heading: Or use the context options
steps.body: Alternatively, open the parent page, check the lower context options drawer, and select Add Child Page.

steps.heading: Title and write your page
steps.body: Type a descriptive, search-optimized title and write out your sub-section content.

steps.heading: Check permissions
steps.body: Adjust document restrictions in the top right and confirm content security inherits properly from the parent.

steps.heading: Publish
steps.body: Click Publish to make your new child page live within the nested structure.

faq.question: Will a child page automatically inherit its parent page's permissions?
faq.answer: Yes, sub-pages can be set to automatically adopt viewing security from their parent page, though you should confirm this in the restrictions panel.

faq.question: Can I turn an existing standalone page into a child page?
faq.answer: Yes, you can reorder pages to change a standalone page into a nested sub-node or move it between spaces.

faq.question: How many levels deep can child pages go?
faq.answer: Confluence supports multi-tier nesting, so child pages can have their own child pages to build a deep hierarchy.

faq.question: Where do I see the full hierarchy of parent and child pages?
faq.answer: The expanded sidebar tree shows multi-tier folder nodes so you can confirm your newly published child pages align correctly.
